risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "We used permuted block randomization at the level of the patient. Once eligibility was determined, written consent obtained, and the baseline interview completed, the study coordinator opened a sealed envelope containing a computer generated random assignment." Comment: This describes a proper random allocation process using permuted blocks and a computer-generated random assignment in sealed envelopes, which is sufficient to judge random sequence generation as low risk.

_evidence.method_quote: We used permuted block randomization at the level of the patient. Once eligibility was determined, written consent obtained, and the baseline interview completed, the study coordinator opened a sealed envelope containing a computer generated random assignment.
_evidence.method_summary: Patients were assigned using permuted block randomization with a computer-generated random assignment in sealed envelopes.
context_clues: randomized to one of the 4 study arms

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Once eligibility was determined, written consent obtained, and the baseline interview completed, the study coordinator opened a sealed envelope containing a computer generated random assignment." Comment: The article reports use of a sealed envelope and computer-generated assignment, but it does not explicitly state that the envelopes were sequentially numbered and opaque. Because SNOSE requires all three features (sequentially numbered, opaque, sealed) to prevent foreknowledge, allocation concealment cannot be confirmed as low risk from the text provided.

_evidence.concealment_quote: Once eligibility was determined, written consent obtained, and the baseline interview completed, the study coordinator opened a sealed envelope containing a computer generated random assignment.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Clinical and study staff and study participants were all blinded to assignment until after the baseline interview. The nature of the intervention meant that participants were not blind to the condition after participation in the intervention." Comment: Blinding was initially in place for both participants and staff, and the loss of blinding occurred only after the intervention had been delivered. Because the main outcomes include several objective outcomes (e.g. referral, orthopedic appointment, receipt of TKR) and the article does not indicate that post-intervention awareness plausibly compromised delivery in a way that would bias the dominant outcome set, the overall performance-bias risk is low.

_evidence.blinding_quote: Clinical and study staff and study participants were all blinded to assignment until after the baseline interview. The nature of the intervention meant that participants were not blind to the condition after participation in the intervention.
_evidence.blinding_method: sealed envelope containing a computer generated random assignment; participants and staff blinded only until after the baseline interview; no sham for the active intervention, attention control received a patient educational booklet
outcome_categories: willingness to consider total knee replacement
knowledge regarding knee OA and joint replacement
subjective_outcomes: willingness to consider total knee replacement
knowledge regarding knee OA and joint replacement

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The nature of the intervention meant that participants were not blind to the condition after participation in the intervention." and "Participant interviews were completed at baseline and at 1, 3 & 12 months following the intervention..." Comment: The key outcomes include multiple patient-reported measures (willingness to consider TKR, knowledge items, knee expectations, discussion with PCP) assessed by participant interview, so the patient was effectively the outcome assessor. Because participants were unblinded after the intervention, knowledge of allocation could influence these subjective/self-reported outcomes. Although referral/appointment/TKR were validated by chart review and are more objective, the worst-outcome rule applies: the unblinded patient-reported outcomes make the domain high risk overall.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: Clinical and study staff and study participants were all blinded to assignment until after the baseline interview. The nature of the intervention meant that participants were not blind to the condition after participation in the intervention.

outcomes.label: discussion with PCP about knee pain

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "There were no losses to follow-up except for one patient in the MI arm and one patient in DA/MI arm. Over the course of the study 93% of the subjects completed at least 2 of the 3 follow-up interviews with no differences among the 4 intervention groups (p=0.62)." Comment: The reported post-randomization attrition on the analysed outcome appears very low (2 losses among 639 randomized, with one loss in each of two active arms and no evidence of meaningful arm imbalance). The authors also used mixed effect regressions / mixed effect logistic regressions, which is compatible with appropriate handling of remaining missing data. This supports low risk of attrition bias.

_evidence.missing_data_method: mixed effect regressions; mixed effect logistic regressions
_evidence.notes: Article reports: '639 participants met the study criteria and were randomized'; 'There were 24 patients who received the baseline assessment but never received the intervention and were not included in the final analysis.' It also states 'There were no losses to follow-up except for one patient in the MI arm and one patient in DA/MI arm.' No per-arm analysed denominators are given in the text or tables.
